Tutorial for the quality control of climate data with agrometflow

Author

agrometflow

Published

April 2, 2026

1 Introduction

This page illustrates how to use the agrometflow plotting functions for visual quality control of climate data. These functions are inspired by the R package dataresqc by Yuri Brugnara & Stefan Hunziker.

The functions covered here are:

Function Description
plot_daily Daily time series with missing-data indicators
plot_decimals Distribution of decimal digits (reporting resolution)
plot_weekly_cycle Wet-day fraction by day of week (data-rescue bias test)
plot_subdaily Sub-daily data organized by month

All functions display inline in Jupyter/Quarto by default (show=True) and can optionally save to PDF (outfile= or outpath=).

2 Setup

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pathlib import Path
from agrometflow.dataquality.plots import (
    plot_decimals,
    plot_daily,
    plot_subdaily,
    plot_weekly_cycle
)

3 Load sample data

We use a daily climate series downloaded from NASA POWER for a location in West Africa (lat 12.5°N, lon -1.2°E), covering 1981–2020.

csv_path = Path("data/climate/power/power_19810101_20201231.csv")

clim_df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
clim_df["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(clim_df["Date"], format="%Y%m%d", errors="coerce")

print(f"Records : {len(clim_df)}")
print(f"Period  : {clim_df['Date'].min().date()} → {clim_df['Date'].max().date()}")
print(f"Columns : {list(clim_df.columns)}")
clim_df.head()
Records : 14610
Period  : 1981-01-01 → 2020-12-31
Columns : ['Date', 'T2M', 'PR', 'TMIN', 'TMAX', 'lat', 'lon']
Date T2M PR TMIN TMAX lat lon
0 1981-01-01 20.17 0.0 12.58 30.38 12.5 -1.2
1 1981-01-02 19.59 0.0 11.99 30.16 12.5 -1.2
2 1981-01-03 18.53 0.0 11.05 28.55 12.5 -1.2
3 1981-01-04 19.11 0.0 11.84 28.83 12.5 -1.2
4 1981-01-05 19.46 0.0 12.48 29.25 12.5 -1.2

4 Daily time series

The function plot_daily plots a daily time series with one panel per year (or group of years). Missing dates are marked with red crosses at the bottom of each panel.

4.1 One year per panel

plot_daily(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Tmax",
    units="°C",
    startyear=2010,
    endyear=2012,
    years_per_panel=1,
    show_missing=True,
    show=True,
    color="steelblue",
    linewidth=0.8
)

4.2 Multiple years per panel

You can group several years in a single panel using years_per_panel:

plot_daily(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Tmax",
    units="°C",
    startyear=1981,
    endyear=2020,
    years_per_panel=5,
    show_missing=False,
    show=True,
    color="darkorange",
    linewidth=0.6,
    alpha=0.8
)

5 Distribution of decimals

The function plot_decimals examines the reporting resolution of a series by counting how often each decimal digit (0–9) appears in the data.

A uniform distribution across all decimals indicates 0.1-unit precision. Systematic over- or under-representation of certain decimals is a sign of rounding, conversion artefacts, or digitization biases.

plot_decimals(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    startyear=1981,
    endyear=2020,
    show=True
)
<Figure size 960x480 with 0 Axes>
Note

In this NASA POWER example, the decimals are distributed fairly uniformly — expected for a gridded reanalysis product. For digitized station data, you would typically see spikes at .0 or .5 (0.5°C or 1°C resolution).

6 Weekly cycle in precipitation

The function plot_weekly_cycle tests whether wet-day frequency varies systematically by day of week using a binomial test (Hunziker et al., 2017).

This is useful for detecting data-rescue biases: if a human observer tended to skip or accumulate observations on certain days of the week, a systematic pattern would appear.

Bars shown in red indicate a statistically significant deviation from the overall wet-day probability (dashed line).

results = plot_weekly_cycle(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "PR"]].rename(columns={"PR": "rr"}),
    date_col="Date",
    precip_col="rr",
    station_id="POWER_station",
    threshold=1.0,
    confidence_level=0.95,
    show=True
)

print(f"\nOverall wet-day probability φ = {results['phi']:.3f}")
print("\nSignificant deviations by day of week:")
found = False
for wd, sig, frac in zip(results["weekdays"], results["significant"], results["fractions"]):
    if sig:
        direction = "↑" if frac > results["phi"] else "↓"
        print(f"  {wd}: {frac:.3f} {direction}")
        found = True
if not found:
    print("  None — no significant weekly cycle detected.")

Overall wet-day probability φ = 0.375

Significant deviations by day of week:
  None — no significant weekly cycle detected.

7 Sub-daily data

The function plot_subdaily plots sub-daily observations organized by month, with one panel per month (4 × 3 grid). This is useful for inspecting digitized historical sub-daily data for transcription errors, time-of-day biases, or systematic shifts.

Here we illustrate with synthetic hourly temperature data following a realistic annual + diurnal cycle:

dates = pd.date_range("2020-01-01", "2020-12-31 23:00", freq="h")
np.random.seed(42)
synthetic_temp = (
    20
    + 10 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * np.arange(len(dates)) / (24 * 365) - np.pi / 2)
    +  6 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * np.arange(len(dates)) / 24 - np.pi / 2)
    +  np.random.randn(len(dates)) * 1.5
)

subdaily_df = pd.DataFrame({"datetime": dates, "value": synthetic_temp})

plot_subdaily(
    data=subdaily_df,
    datetime_col="datetime",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Temperature (synthetic)",
    units="°C",
    year=2020,
    fixed_ylim=True,
    show=True,
    marker=".",
    markersize=1,
    linestyle="",
    alpha=0.5,
    color="royalblue"
)

8 Save plots to PDF

All functions accept an optional outfile (or outpath for plot_weekly_cycle) parameter to save the plot to a PDF in addition to displaying it.

# Example: save decimals plot to PDF and display inline
out_dir = Path("data/qc")
out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

plot_decimals(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    startyear=2000,
    endyear=2020,
    outfile=out_dir / "decimals_TMAX.pdf",
    show=True
)
Plot saved in data/qc/decimals_TMAX.pdf

9 GHCN-Daily station data

The GHCNDDownloader fetches station observations from the NOAA GHCN-Daily database — the largest archive of daily climate records (~120 000 stations worldwide).

9.1 Search available stations

from agrometflow.climate.ghcnd import GHCNDDownloader

dl = GHCNDDownloader(verbose=True)

# Search stations inside a bounding box (West Africa example)
bbox = (-5.0, 10.0, 5.0, 16.0)   # (min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat)
stations = dl.search_stations(bbox=bbox)
print(f"Stations found: {len(stations)}")
stations.head(10)
Stations found: 29
station_id lat lon elevation name country
0 BN000005306 11.130 2.930 290.0 KANDI BN
1 BN000005319 10.320 1.480 460.0 NATITINGOU BN
2 BN000065306 11.133 2.933 292.0 KANDI BN
3 GHM00065401 10.900 -1.100 203.0 NAVRONGO GH
4 GHM00065404 10.083 -2.508 323.1 WA GH
5 ML000061250 15.867 2.217 278.0 MENAKA ML
6 MLM00061240 15.333 -1.683 288.0 HOMBORI ML
7 MLM00061265 14.513 -4.080 276.1 AMBODEDJO ML
8 MLM00061277 13.333 -4.833 284.0 SAN ML
9 NG000001036 14.220 1.450 209.0 TILLABERY NG

9.2 Check variable inventory

Before downloading, inspect which variables and years are available:

inventory = dl.get_inventory()

# Filter to our bounding box stations and core temperature variables
sids = stations["station_id"].tolist()
inv_sub = inventory[
    inventory["station_id"].isin(sids)
    & inventory["variable"].isin(["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
].sort_values(["station_id", "variable"])

print(f"Inventory rows: {len(inv_sub)}")
inv_sub.head(15)
Inventory rows: 87
station_id lat lon variable firstyear lastyear
63648 BN000005306 11.130 2.930 PRCP 1945 1979
63646 BN000005306 11.130 2.930 TMAX 1945 1979
63647 BN000005306 11.130 2.930 TMIN 1945 1979
63651 BN000005319 10.320 1.480 PRCP 1945 2025
63649 BN000005319 10.320 1.480 TMAX 1945 2025
63650 BN000005319 10.320 1.480 TMIN 1945 2025
63665 BN000065306 11.133 2.933 PRCP 1973 2025
63663 BN000065306 11.133 2.933 TMAX 1973 2025
63664 BN000065306 11.133 2.933 TMIN 1973 2025
128934 GHM00065401 10.900 -1.100 PRCP 1973 2025
128932 GHM00065401 10.900 -1.100 TMAX 1973 2025
128933 GHM00065401 10.900 -1.100 TMIN 1973 2025
128938 GHM00065404 10.083 -2.508 PRCP 1973 2025
128936 GHM00065404 10.083 -2.508 TMAX 1973 2025
128937 GHM00065404 10.083 -2.508 TMIN 1973 2025

9.3 Download by station ID

# Pick one station as an example
station_id = stations.iloc[0]["station_id"]
print(f"Downloading station: {station_id}")

dl.download(
    station_ids=[station_id],
    start_date="2000-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"],
    output_dir="data/ghcnd",
)

df_station = dl.extract(variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
print(f"Records: {len(df_station)}")
df_station.head()

9.4 Download all stations in a bounding box

dl_bbox = GHCNDDownloader()

dl_bbox.download(
    bbox=bbox,
    start_date="2010-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"],
    output_dir="data/ghcnd",
    min_years=5,          # skip stations with < 5 years of overlap
    max_workers=8,
)

df_bbox = dl_bbox.extract(variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
print(f"Stations: {df_bbox['station'].nunique()}")
print(f"Records : {len(df_bbox)}")
df_bbox.head()

9.5 Plot downloaded GHCN-Daily data

Once downloaded, the output CSV can be read back and plotted with the same functions as POWER data:

from pathlib import Path
from agrometflow.climate.ghcnd import GHCNDDownloader
from agrometflow.dataquality.plots import plot_daily, plot_decimals

dl = GHCNDDownloader()
df = dl.extract(source="data/ghcnd/ghcnd_20100101_20201231.csv",
                variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])

# Plot daily TMAX for first station
sid = df["station"].iloc[0]
df_s = df[df["station"] == sid][["date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"})

plot_daily(
    data=df_s,
    date_col="date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name=f"Tmax — {sid}",
    units="°C",
    show=True,
    color="tomato",
    linewidth=0.8,
)

plot_decimals(
    data=df_s,
    date_col="date",
    value_col="value",
    show=True,
)

9.6 Variable reference

Code Description Unit (after conversion)
TMAX Maximum temperature °C
TMIN Minimum temperature °C
TAVG Average temperature °C
PRCP Precipitation mm
SNOW Snowfall mm
SNWD Snow depth mm
AWND Average wind speed m/s
Tip

Raw GHCN-Daily values for temperature and precipitation are stored in tenths of the unit. GHCNDDownloader divides them by 10 automatically when convert_units=True (the default).

10 References

  • Hunziker, S., et al. (2017). Errors in climate data with emphasis on the European region. Int. J. Climatol., 37, 4131–4145. https://doi.org/10.1002/joc.5001

  • Hunziker, S., et al. (2018). Identifying, attributing, and overcoming common data quality issues. Clim. Past, 14, 1–20. https://doi.org/10.5194/cp-14-1-2018

  • dataresqc R package: https://github.com/c3s-data-rescue-service/dataresqc

## Introduction

This page illustrates how to use the **agrometflow** plotting functions for visual
quality control of climate data. These functions are inspired by the R package
[dataresqc](https://github.com/c3s-data-rescue-service/dataresqc) by Yuri Brugnara & Stefan Hunziker.

The functions covered here are:

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `plot_daily` | Daily time series with missing-data indicators |
| `plot_decimals` | Distribution of decimal digits (reporting resolution) |
| `plot_weekly_cycle` | Wet-day fraction by day of week (data-rescue bias test) |
| `plot_subdaily` | Sub-daily data organized by month |

All functions display inline in Jupyter/Quarto by default (`show=True`) and
can optionally save to PDF (`outfile=` or `outpath=`).

---

## Setup

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pathlib import Path
from agrometflow.dataquality.plots import (
    plot_decimals,
    plot_daily,
    plot_subdaily,
    plot_weekly_cycle
)
```

---

## Load sample data

We use a daily climate series downloaded from [NASA POWER](https://power.larc.nasa.gov/)
for a location in West Africa (lat 12.5°N, lon -1.2°E), covering 1981–2020.

```{python}
csv_path = Path("data/climate/power/power_19810101_20201231.csv")

clim_df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
clim_df["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(clim_df["Date"], format="%Y%m%d", errors="coerce")

print(f"Records : {len(clim_df)}")
print(f"Period  : {clim_df['Date'].min().date()} → {clim_df['Date'].max().date()}")
print(f"Columns : {list(clim_df.columns)}")
clim_df.head()
```

---

## Daily time series {#sec-daily}

The function `plot_daily` plots a daily time series with one panel per year
(or group of years). Missing dates are marked with red crosses at the bottom
of each panel.

### One year per panel

```{python}
plot_daily(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Tmax",
    units="°C",
    startyear=2010,
    endyear=2012,
    years_per_panel=1,
    show_missing=True,
    show=True,
    color="steelblue",
    linewidth=0.8
)
```

### Multiple years per panel

You can group several years in a single panel using `years_per_panel`:

```{python}
plot_daily(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Tmax",
    units="°C",
    startyear=1981,
    endyear=2020,
    years_per_panel=5,
    show_missing=False,
    show=True,
    color="darkorange",
    linewidth=0.6,
    alpha=0.8
)
```

---

## Distribution of decimals {#sec-decimals}

The function `plot_decimals` examines the **reporting resolution** of a series
by counting how often each decimal digit (0–9) appears in the data.

A uniform distribution across all decimals indicates 0.1-unit precision.
Systematic over- or under-representation of certain decimals is a sign of
rounding, conversion artefacts, or digitization biases.

```{python}
plot_decimals(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    startyear=1981,
    endyear=2020,
    show=True
)
```

::: {.callout-note}
In this NASA POWER example, the decimals are distributed fairly uniformly —
expected for a gridded reanalysis product. For digitized station data, you
would typically see spikes at `.0` or `.5` (0.5°C or 1°C resolution).
:::

---

## Weekly cycle in precipitation {#sec-weekly}

The function `plot_weekly_cycle` tests whether wet-day frequency varies
systematically by day of week using a **binomial test** (Hunziker et al., 2017).

This is useful for detecting data-rescue biases: if a human observer tended
to skip or accumulate observations on certain days of the week, a systematic
pattern would appear.

Bars shown in **red** indicate a statistically significant deviation from the
overall wet-day probability (dashed line).

```{python}
results = plot_weekly_cycle(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "PR"]].rename(columns={"PR": "rr"}),
    date_col="Date",
    precip_col="rr",
    station_id="POWER_station",
    threshold=1.0,
    confidence_level=0.95,
    show=True
)

print(f"\nOverall wet-day probability φ = {results['phi']:.3f}")
print("\nSignificant deviations by day of week:")
found = False
for wd, sig, frac in zip(results["weekdays"], results["significant"], results["fractions"]):
    if sig:
        direction = "↑" if frac > results["phi"] else "↓"
        print(f"  {wd}: {frac:.3f} {direction}")
        found = True
if not found:
    print("  None — no significant weekly cycle detected.")
```

---

## Sub-daily data {#sec-subdaily}

The function `plot_subdaily` plots sub-daily observations organized by month,
with one panel per month (4 × 3 grid). This is useful for inspecting
digitized historical sub-daily data for transcription errors, time-of-day
biases, or systematic shifts.

Here we illustrate with synthetic hourly temperature data following a realistic
annual + diurnal cycle:

```{python}
dates = pd.date_range("2020-01-01", "2020-12-31 23:00", freq="h")
np.random.seed(42)
synthetic_temp = (
    20
    + 10 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * np.arange(len(dates)) / (24 * 365) - np.pi / 2)
    +  6 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * np.arange(len(dates)) / 24 - np.pi / 2)
    +  np.random.randn(len(dates)) * 1.5
)

subdaily_df = pd.DataFrame({"datetime": dates, "value": synthetic_temp})

plot_subdaily(
    data=subdaily_df,
    datetime_col="datetime",
    value_col="value",
    var_name="Temperature (synthetic)",
    units="°C",
    year=2020,
    fixed_ylim=True,
    show=True,
    marker=".",
    markersize=1,
    linestyle="",
    alpha=0.5,
    color="royalblue"
)
```

---

## Save plots to PDF

All functions accept an optional `outfile` (or `outpath` for `plot_weekly_cycle`)
parameter to save the plot to a PDF in addition to displaying it.

```{python}
# Example: save decimals plot to PDF and display inline
out_dir = Path("data/qc")
out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

plot_decimals(
    data=clim_df[["Date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"}),
    date_col="Date",
    value_col="value",
    startyear=2000,
    endyear=2020,
    outfile=out_dir / "decimals_TMAX.pdf",
    show=True
)
```

---

## GHCN-Daily station data {#sec-ghcnd}

The `GHCNDDownloader` fetches station observations from the
[NOAA GHCN-Daily](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/land-based-station/global-historical-climatology-network-daily)
database — the largest archive of daily climate records (~120 000 stations worldwide).

### Search available stations

```{python}
from agrometflow.climate.ghcnd import GHCNDDownloader

dl = GHCNDDownloader(verbose=True)

# Search stations inside a bounding box (West Africa example)
bbox = (-5.0, 10.0, 5.0, 16.0)   # (min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat)
stations = dl.search_stations(bbox=bbox)
print(f"Stations found: {len(stations)}")
stations.head(10)
```

### Check variable inventory

Before downloading, inspect which variables and years are available:

```{python}
inventory = dl.get_inventory()

# Filter to our bounding box stations and core temperature variables
sids = stations["station_id"].tolist()
inv_sub = inventory[
    inventory["station_id"].isin(sids)
    & inventory["variable"].isin(["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
].sort_values(["station_id", "variable"])

print(f"Inventory rows: {len(inv_sub)}")
inv_sub.head(15)
```

### Download by station ID

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Pick one station as an example
station_id = stations.iloc[0]["station_id"]
print(f"Downloading station: {station_id}")

dl.download(
    station_ids=[station_id],
    start_date="2000-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"],
    output_dir="data/ghcnd",
)

df_station = dl.extract(variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
print(f"Records: {len(df_station)}")
df_station.head()
```

### Download all stations in a bounding box

```{python}
#| eval: false
dl_bbox = GHCNDDownloader()

dl_bbox.download(
    bbox=bbox,
    start_date="2010-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"],
    output_dir="data/ghcnd",
    min_years=5,          # skip stations with < 5 years of overlap
    max_workers=8,
)

df_bbox = dl_bbox.extract(variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])
print(f"Stations: {df_bbox['station'].nunique()}")
print(f"Records : {len(df_bbox)}")
df_bbox.head()
```

### Plot downloaded GHCN-Daily data

Once downloaded, the output CSV can be read back and plotted with the same
functions as POWER data:

```{python}
#| eval: false
from pathlib import Path
from agrometflow.climate.ghcnd import GHCNDDownloader
from agrometflow.dataquality.plots import plot_daily, plot_decimals

dl = GHCNDDownloader()
df = dl.extract(source="data/ghcnd/ghcnd_20100101_20201231.csv",
                variables=["TMAX", "TMIN", "PRCP"])

# Plot daily TMAX for first station
sid = df["station"].iloc[0]
df_s = df[df["station"] == sid][["date", "TMAX"]].rename(columns={"TMAX": "value"})

plot_daily(
    data=df_s,
    date_col="date",
    value_col="value",
    var_name=f"Tmax — {sid}",
    units="°C",
    show=True,
    color="tomato",
    linewidth=0.8,
)

plot_decimals(
    data=df_s,
    date_col="date",
    value_col="value",
    show=True,
)
```

### Variable reference

| Code | Description | Unit (after conversion) |
|------|-------------|------------------------|
| `TMAX` | Maximum temperature | °C |
| `TMIN` | Minimum temperature | °C |
| `TAVG` | Average temperature | °C |
| `PRCP` | Precipitation | mm |
| `SNOW` | Snowfall | mm |
| `SNWD` | Snow depth | mm |
| `AWND` | Average wind speed | m/s |

::: {.callout-tip}
Raw GHCN-Daily values for temperature and precipitation are stored in tenths
of the unit. `GHCNDDownloader` divides them by 10 automatically when
`convert_units=True` (the default).
:::

---

## References

- Hunziker, S., et al. (2017). Errors in climate data with emphasis on the
  European region. *Int. J. Climatol.*, 37, 4131–4145.
  <https://doi.org/10.1002/joc.5001>

- Hunziker, S., et al. (2018). Identifying, attributing, and overcoming
  common data quality issues. *Clim. Past*, 14, 1–20.
  <https://doi.org/10.5194/cp-14-1-2018>

- dataresqc R package: <https://github.com/c3s-data-rescue-service/dataresqc>
